moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the Queen's Gambit, one of the most fundamental openings in chess. White plays 1.d4, claiming the center and preparing the c4 advance that defines the Gambit.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Single d4 pawn controls e5 and c5. White aims to expand with c4 next.
plans: Follow up with c4 to build an ideal pawn center
Develop pieces behind the pawn chain for a strong middlegame
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1.d4 claims the center, preparing c4 to challenge Black's d5.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds with d5, matching White's central claim. The d5 pawn controls c4 and e4, establishing a direct fight for the center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Symmetrical d4 vs d5. The central tension will define the opening battle.
plans: Maintain the d5 pawn and develop pieces naturally
Look for ...c5 to challenge White's center when the time is right
alternatives: 1...Nf6 (Indian Defenses) — flexible, avoids committing the d-pawn early
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's d5 matches White's central claim, controlling c4 and e4 to fight for the center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays c4, the Queen's Gambit. The c4 pawn challenges d5, offering a trade that would give White the ideal d4+e4 center. Despite the name, it's not a true gambit — White can always recover the pawn.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White has pawns on d4 and c4, pressuring Black's d5 from the side.
plans: If Black takes on c4, recapture or play e4 for a big center
If Black holds d5, build pressure with Nc3 and prepare to exchange on d5
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: c4 challenges d5 for the ideal d4+e4 center, and White can always recover the pawn.
